concepts.title: Physical properties of group 14
concepts.conceptBody:
GROUP-14

Elements of this group are p-block element. Carbon and Si are non-metals (Si slightly metallic), Ge is metalloid, $\text{Sn}$ and $\text{Pb}$ are metals.

Carbon has three isotopes, they are:

Physical properties of group-14 elements

{The values are not to be remembered, these are given to show the variations}

Note: 

(i) The radii of $\text{Si}$ and Ge are very similar due to d-block contraction. Also $\text{Sn}$ and $\text{Pb}$ have almost similar radii due to lanthanide contraction.

(ii) Ionisation energy vary as $\text{C}>\text{Si}\sim\text{Ge}>\text{Sn}<\text{Pb}$. Silicon and Ge have comparable IE due to similarity in their radii.

The radius of $\text{Sn}$ is smaller than $\text{Pb}$ but $\text{Pb}$ has higher $\text{E}$ than $\text{Sn}$. It is due to higher $\text{Z}*$ for $\text{Pb}$ as $F$ - orbital is least shielding.

concepts.title: Oxidation state and Catenation
concepts.conceptBody:
Oxidation states and Bonding

Group-14 elements show variable oxidation states of +2 and +4 . The stability of +2 state is very stable for $\text{Pb}$. It is due to inert pair effect.

Carbon and Si have high ionisation energy. Therefore, they are covalent in their compounds. Tin and $\text{Pb}$ form ionic compounds having appreciable covalent nature. It is because $\text{Sn}$ and $\text{Pb}$ are not very large and have $18\text{e}$ core.

Carbon and $\text{Si}$ differ widely in their bonding pattern. It is because of the following reasons:

The features of certain $\text{Si}$ compounds having $\text{Si}-\text{O}$ and $\text{Si}-\text{N}$ bonds are explained by $(\text{d}-\text{p})$ $\pi $ bonding. For example,

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} {{\left( \text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}} \right)}_{3}}~\text{N} & \text{ triangular Pyramidal } \\ {{\left( \text{Si}{{\text{H}}_{3}} \right)}_{3}}~\text{N} & \text{ triangular Planar } \\ {{\left( \text{Si}{{\text{H}}_{3}} \right)}_{2}}\text{O} & <(\text{Si}-\text{O}-\text{Si})\approx 144 \\ \end{array}$

Trisilylamine, ${{\left( \text{Si}{{\text{H}}_{3}} \right)}_{3}}~\text{N}$, is trigonal planar due to $(\text{d}-\text{p})\pi $ bonding involving filled $\text{N}2{{\text{p}}_{\text{z}}}$ orbital and vacant $\text{Si}3~{{\text{d}}_{\text{xy}}}$ orbitals.

The large angles at $\text{O}$ of disilyl ethers $\left( {{\text{R}}_{3}}\text{S}{{\text{i}}_{2}}_{2}\text{O} \right.$, is due both to electronic and steric factors.

Catenation

The property of self-linking is known as catenation. It depends mainly on bond energy. The $\text{C}-\text{C}$ bond energy is very high. Therefore, $\text{C}$ has maximum catenation property. The bond energy decreases down the group and so also the catenation property, i.e.

$\text{C}\gg \text{Si}>\text{Ge}\approx \text{Sn}\gg \text{Pb}$

Sulphur also shows catenation because $\text{S}-\text{S}$ bond energy is relatively high. Boron, $\text{Si},\text{P}$ shows heterocatenation as $\text{B}-\text{O},\text{Si}-\text{O}$ and $\text{P}-\text{O}$ bond energies are high.

concepts.title: Allotropes of Carbon Graphite
concepts.conceptBody:
Allotropy and structure

All the elements are solid. Carbon and Sn only show allotropy.

Carbon has three crystalline allotropes:

(a) Graphite

(b) Diamond and

(c) Fullerene.

Tin is found as $\alpha -\text{Sn, and }\beta -\text{Sn}\text{. }$

(a) Graphite

It is soft dull looking greasy substance and is electrical conductor. Graphite has two-dimensional layer structure.

Layers are made up of $\text{s}{{\text{p}}^{2}}$ hybridized $\text{C}$ atoms. Each $\text{C}$ is joined with three other $\text{C}$ atoms through $\sigma -$ bonds forming hexagonal rings. Each $\text{C}$ is left with its fourth valence electron in a p-orbital. They overlap and form $\pi $ bonds. These $\pi $ bonds are delocalized. Therefore, all $\text{C}-\text{C}$ bond distances in graphite are equal $(1.415{Å})$. Sheet to sheet distance in graphite is $3.35{Å}$. It is known as $\text{C}$ - dimension. Layers are joined by weak Van der walls force.

The weak force between layers allows one layer to slide over the other. It imparts softness to graphite. It is, therefore, used in pencils for writing. Due to delocalized $\pi $ electrons graphite is a conductor (though along the sheet not perpendicular to it). Hence, it is used as electrode.

concepts.title: Diamond
concepts.conceptBody:
(b) Diamond

It is colourless transparent hardest substance known and is insulator. Cut diamond is sparkling solid (due to refraction). Diamond has cubic unit cell. Each $\text{C}$ is $\text{s}{{\text{p}}^{3}}$ hybridised and is joined with four other $\text{C}$ atoms through $\sigma $ - bonds.

As $\text{C}-\text{C}$ bond energy $\left( 356\text{KJmo}{{\text{l}}^{-1}} \right.$ ) is very high and diamond has a giant structure, it is very hard. This structure is responsible for its insulator property.

Density of diamond $(3.51~\text{g}/\text{cc})$ is greater than graphite $(2.22~\text{g}/\text{cc})$. This shows that to convert graphite into diamond, pressure must be applied. There is the equilibrium.

$\text{ Graphite }\text{ diamond }$

Graphite can be transformed into diamond at a reasonable rate at high pressure and $\sim300~\text{K}$ in the presence of a transition metal $(\text{Fe},\text{Cr},\text{Pt})$ catalyst.

concepts.title: Fullerenes
concepts.conceptBody:
(c) Fullerenes

Fullerenes are carbon clusters having cage like structures.

Fullerenes ${{\text{C}}_{60}}$ and ${{\text{C}}_{70}}$ can be separated by chromatogrphic method over alumina. Many other fullerenes also exist such as ${{\text{C}}_{32}},{{\text{C}}_{50}},{{\text{C}}_{76}},{{\text{C}}_{84}}$ etc.

The fullerenes ${{\text{C}}_{60}}$ is well known. It has a soccer ball structure which contains 12 five - membered and 20 six membered rings. The rings are unsaturated. The ${{\text{C}}_{60}}$ fullerene is called Buckminster fullerene in honour of American architect Robert Buckminster Fuller.

Structure of${{\mathbf{C}}_{\mathbf{60}}}$

Silicon exists only in diamond structure. A graphite like structure for$\text{Si}$ is not possible because it has no propensity to form $(p-p)\pi $ bond.

Tin exists in three forms, $\alpha -\text{Sn},\beta -\text{Sn}$ and $\gamma -\text{Sn}$.

concepts.title: Semiconductor property of Si and Ge
concepts.conceptBody:
Semiconductor property of Si and Ge

Silicon and to a less extent Ge are important semiconductor materials. Pure $\text{Si}$ and Ge are non-conductor of electricity. However, when they are dopped with group -13 or group -15 elements, they become semiconductor

(i) Ge dopped with In

$\text{Ge }-4{{s}^{2}}4s{{p}^{2}}\text{ }$

$\text{ In}-5{{s}^{2}}5{{p}^{1}}$

When Ge is dopped with In, there is one electron less at In site. There develops a positive hole in the lattice which makes Ge conducting. Such a semiconductor is called p - type (positive - type) semiconductor.

(ii) Ge dopped with As

$\text{ Ge }-4{{s}^{2}}4{{p}^{2}}\text{ }$

$\text{As }-4{{s}^{2}}4{{p}^{3}}$

When As is dopped in Ge, there is one electron excess at As site. Due to this excess electron Ge conducts electricity. Such a semiconductor is called $n-$ type (negative type).

concepts.title: Multiple Bonding
concepts.conceptBody:
Multiple Bonding

The tendency of formation of multiple bonds easily, in case of carbon, is because of the high bond energy and a small atomic radius of carbon is compared to other elements of the group. So its p-orbitals can approach the p-orbitals of the other $\text{C}$, $\text{O}$ or $\text{N}$ atom for an effective overlap essential for $\pi $-bond formation.

  • Silicon also uses d-orbitals in multiple bonding, In case of $\mathbf{N(C}{{\mathbf{H}}_{\mathbf{3}}}{{\mathbf{)}}_{\mathbf{3}}}\mathbf{,}$ geometry is pyramidal, but in case of $\mathbf{N(}Si{{\mathbf{H}}_{\mathbf{3}}}{{\mathbf{)}}_{\mathbf{3}}}$ it is planar. It is due to the fact that in the latter, the lone pair of N- atom is transferred to empty d-orbitals of silicon ($\mathbf{p\pi -d\pi }$ overlapping) : 

The normal valency of the elements is four, but apart from carbon, the elements can make more than four bonds. This is because they make use of a set of d-orbitals in bonding. Thus availability of d-orbitals (empty but electrons of $\text{n}{{\text{s}}^{2}}$ and $\text{n}{{\text{p}}^{2}}$ can be excited into this) is responsible for the ability of the elements, except carbon, to make complex ions such as $\text{Si}{{\text{F}}_{6}}^{2-}$.

Fig. Energy difference between $2\text{p}$ and $3~\text{d}$ energy levels in carbon 
and between $3\text{p}$ and $3~\text{d}$ energy levels in silicon.

  • The main chemical differences between silicon and carbon are:
  • Si does not form stable double bonds
  • Si does not form very stable $\text{Si}-\text{Si}$ bonds.
  • Si has vacant 3d-orbitals in its valence shell into which it can accept electrons from donor atoms.
  • Some carbon compound are less reactive than the corresponding compounds of the other members of the group. $\text{CC}{{\text{l}}_{4}}$ has no interaction with ${{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$ (hydrolysis) whereas $\text{SiC}{{\text{l}}_{4}}$ does by use of empty 3d-orbitals.
  • All the elements give hydrides. Carbon, of course gives an immense number, but silicon and germanium also show a wide variety.

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \text{C}{{\text{H}}_{4}} & \text{ methane (alkane) } \\ \text{Sn}{{\text{H}}_{4}} & \text{ Stannane } \\ \text{Si}{{\text{H}}_{4}} & \text{ silane } \\ \text{Pb}{{\text{H}}_{4}} & \text{ plumbane } \\ \text{Ge}{{\text{H}}_{4}} & \text{ germane } \\ \end{array}$

Carbon hydrides will not ignite in air unless a flame is put to them. Apart from silane, $\text{Si}{{\text{H}}_{4}}$, the silicon hydrides are less well behaved. $\text{S}{{\text{i}}_{3}}{{\text{H}}_{8}}$ is spontaneously flammable in air:

$\text{S}{{\text{i}}_{3}}{{\text{H}}_{8}}(\text{l})+5{{\text{O}}_{2}}(~\text{g})\xrightarrow{{}}3\text{Si}{{\text{O}}_{2}}(~\text{s})+4{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}(\text{l})$

Alkanes as well as silane are not hydrolysed however, traces of alkali converts silane into hydrated silica $\text{Si}{{\text{O}}_{2}}\cdot \text{n}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$.

  • As we descend the group, acidic nature of the oxides decreases and basic nature of the oxides increases.

$\text{Si},\text{Ge},\text{Sn}$ and $\text{Pb}$ form oxides $\text{M}{{\text{O}}_{2}}\cdot \text{Si}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$ is infinite three-dimensional network solid of silicon and oxygen atoms connected by single covalent bonds. $\text{Ge}{{\text{O}}_{2}},\text{Sn}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$ and $\text{Pb}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$ are all solids and exist in several modifications.

concepts.title: Ionic carbides
concepts.conceptBody:
(i) Ionic carbides 
Three types of ionic carbides are found:
(a) Methanides,
(b) Acetylides,
(c) Propynide.
Ionic carbides are decomposed by water (or dil. Acid) at room temperature. The liberated anions are also immediately hydrolysed. It affords a method of classification.
(a) Methanides: (${{\mathbf{C}}^{\mathbf{4}}}$ unit) 
Those carbides which are hydrolysed to give $\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{4}}$ are called methanide. They are supposed to contain ${{\text{C}}^{-4}}$ unit.
$\text{B}{{\text{e}}_{2}}\text{C}+4{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O }\xrightarrow{{}}2\text{Be}{{(\text{OH})}_{2}}+\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{4}}$
$\text{A}{{\text{l}}_{4}}{{\text{C}}_{3}}+12{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O }\xrightarrow{{}}4\text{Al}{{(\text{OH})}_{3}}+3\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{4}}$
(b) Acetylides:$\mathbf{(C}_{\mathbf{2}}^{\mathbf{2}}$ unit) 
Those which are hydrolysed to ${{\text{C}}_{2}}{{\text{H}}_{2}}$ are called acetylides. They are common and formed by Group -1 , Group $-2,\text{Mg},\text{Ca},\text{Sr},\text{Ba},\text{Zn},\text{Cd},\text{Cu},\text{Ag},\text{Al}$ and La metals.

$\text{Ca}{{\text{C}}_{2}}+2{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{Ca}{{(\text{OH})}_{2}}+{{\text{C}}_{2}}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{ }$
$\text{A}{{\text{l}}_{2}}{{\left( {{\text{C}}_{2}} \right)}_{3}}+6{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\xrightarrow{{}}2\text{Al}{{(\text{OH})}_{3}}+3{{\text{C}}_{2}}{{\text{H}}_{2}}$
(c) Propynide: $\mathbf{(}{{\mathbf{C}}_{\mathbf{3}}}^{\mathbf{4}}$ unit) 
Such carbides hydrolyse to propyne
$\text{M}{{\text{g}}_{2}}{{\text{C}}_{3}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\xrightarrow{{}}2\text{Mg}{{(\text{OH})}_{2}}+\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}-\text{C}\equiv \text{CH}$

concepts.title: Covalent carbides
concepts.conceptBody:
Covalent carbides 

Elements which compares with$\text{C}$ in size and electronegativity form such carbides, Be, B, Si etc. Boron and Si carbides are more important.

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} \text{Si}{{\text{O}}_{2}}+2\text{C}\xrightarrow{\Delta }\text{Si}+2\text{CO} \\ \text{Si}+\text{C}\xrightarrow{High\,\,temp}\text{SiC} \\ \end{array}$

Silicon carbide ($\text{SiC}$) is called carborundum. It is very inert and extremely hard.

${{\text{B}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{3}}+\text{C}\xrightarrow{High\,temp}{{\text{B}}_{4}}\text{C}$

${{\text{B}}_{4}}\text{C}$ is a also very hard, infusible and inert. It is used to make bulletproof clothing.

These covalent carbides (or $\alpha -\text{A}{{\text{l}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$ ) are comparable in hardness to diamond (Moh's scale).

Hardness \[\xrightarrow{{}}\]Diamond $>{{\text{B}}_{4}}\text{C}>\text{SiC}>\alpha -\text{A}{{\text{l}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$

concepts.title: Interstitial type carbide
concepts.conceptBody:
(iii) Interstitial carbides 
Transition metals form such carbides, in which $\text{C}$ atoms occupy octahedral voids. Such materials are very hard, electrically conducting and have very high mp. For example: TiC, $\text{ZrC},\text{MoC},\text{WC}$ etc.

concepts.title: Carbon Monoxide
concepts.conceptBody:
2. Oxides

Electrons of this form oxides of the type $\text{MO}$ and $\text{M}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$. Carbon forms suboxides also, e.g., ${{\text{C}}_{3}}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$.

(a) Carbon monoxide (CO) 

It forms many important gaseous mixtures of industrial use

(i) Water gas or synthesis gas $\text{CO}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}$

(ii) Producer gas $\text{CO}+{{\text{N}}_{2}}$

(iii) Coal gas $\text{CO}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}+\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{4}}+\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$

$\to $ $\text{CO}$ is a neutral oxide.

$\to $ Carbon monoxide has a triple bonded $\text{C}$ atom, one $\text{s}$ and two $\text{p}$ bonds (best explained by MO model).

$\to $ $\text{CO}$, however, is a strong ligand towards transition metals and form variety of complexes known as carbonyls, $\text{Ni}{{(\text{CO})}_{4}},\text{Fe}{{(\text{CO})}_{5}}$ etc. They are stable due to synergic bonding.

$\to $ $\text{CO}$ is very toxic and rapidly forms a bright red complex with haemoglobin of blood. Thus preventing haemoglobin to carry oxygen to cells, using oxygen deficiency to unconsciousness and then death.

concepts.title: Carbon Dioxide
concepts.conceptBody:
(b) Carbon dioxide $\left( \mathbf{C}{{\mathbf{O}}_{\mathbf{2}}} \right)$ 

$\to $ $\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$ is acidic oxide

$\to $ $\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$ is biologically important

(i) Maintains $\text{pH}$ of blood, due to the equilibrium

$\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\rightleftharpoons \text{HCO}_{3}^{-}+{{\text{H}}^{+}}$

(ii) Main components of photo synthesis

$6\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}+6{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\xrightarrow[Sun\,\,ligh]{Chlorophyll}\underset{\text{ (Glucose) }}{\mathop{{{\text{C}}_{6}}{{\text{H}}_{12}}{{\text{O}}_{6}}}}\,+6{{\text{O}}_{2}}$

(iii) The reverse process of the above takes place in the process of respiration.

${{\text{C}}_{6}}{{\text{H}}_{12}}{{\text{O}}_{6}}+6{{\text{O}}_{2}}\xrightarrow{{}}6\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}+6{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}+\text{ Energy }$

$\to $ $\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$ is used in the synthesis of urea

$\to $ $\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$ is gas at NTP and has the structure $\text{O}=\text{C}=0$. The $(\text{p}-\text{p})\pi $ bonding is very effective. Therefore, even in solid state discrete $\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$ molecules exist. The intermolecular forces are weak Van der Waals' force.

$\to $ Solid $\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$ is called 'dry ice' or 'cardice'.

Silicon dioxide, $\text{Si}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$, unlike $\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$ is a solid. The $(\text{p}-\text{p})\pi $ bonding is not effective in $\text{Si}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$ like $\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$. It is due to larger size of $\text{Si}$ and more core electrons (8e) in $\text{Si}$. Therefore, to stabilize; $\text{Si}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$ contains a continuous lattice of $\text{Si}$ and oxygen atoms joined by covalent bonds. Each $\text{Si}$ is $\text{s}{{\text{p}}^{3}}$ and is joined with $4\text{O}+$ atoms and each $\text{O}$ - atom with two $\text{Si}$ - atoms. A macromolecule is formed, hence $\text{Si}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$ is a solid.
